name: Shankhapushpi
usesInAyurveda: Tridoshic Modulation: It is considered Tridoshahara (balances all three doshas). Its Madhura Rasa and Sheeta Virya pacify Pitta, its Snigdha Guna pacifies Vata, and its Tikta Rasa helps keep Kapha in check. This makes it one of the rare herbs suitable for all constitutional types.

Karma (Actions): Medhya (brain tonic/nootropic), Buddhivardhaka (intellect-promoting), Smritivardhaka (memory-enhancing), Manasrogahara (psychotropic/anti-anxiety), Nidrajanana (mild sleep-inducing), Vayasthapana (anti-aging), Balya (strength-promoting), Unmadahara (anti-psychotic).

API/AFI Adherence: The whole plant (Panchanga) is used, as specified in the Ayurvedic Pharmacopoeia of India (API). It is the primary ingredient in classical formulations like Shankhapushpi Syrup and is part of the famous four Medhya Rasayanas described in Charaka Samhita (Chikitsasthana 1/3).

Specific Indications: Unmada (psychosis), Apasmara (epilepsy), Bhrama (vertigo), Anidra (insomnia), Smriti Daurbalya (poor memory), Buddhi Daurbalya (poor intellect), Chittodvega (anxiety), and Uccha Raktachapa (hypertension).
usageForm: Whole plant powder (Churna), Whole plant juice (Svarasa), Syrup, Decoction (Kwatha), Medicated ghee (Shankhapushpi Ghrita), Medicated oil (Taila).
mainMedicinalUses: Nootropic and Cognitive Enhancement (Medhya Rasayana): Shankhapushpi is universally recognized in Ayurveda as the premier brain tonic. It enhances learning, concentration, memory consolidation, and overall cognitive function. The Charaka Samhita specifically names it as the foremost Medhya Rasayana herb. Pharmacological studies confirm its nootropic activity through cholinergic pathway modulation and neuroprotection.

Anxiolytic and Anti-Depressant: It possesses significant anxiolytic properties by calming the Manas (mind) and stabilizing Prana Vata (the dosha governing mental function). Research has shown its extracts to produce anxiolytic effects comparable to standard anxiolytic drugs in animal models, without sedative side effects at therapeutic doses.

Anti-Epileptic (Apasmara): Traditionally used as a principal remedy for epilepsy. Studies in animal models have demonstrated its anticonvulsant properties, attributed to its GABAergic modulation and neuroprotective mechanisms.

Anti-Hypertensive: Demonstrated mild blood-pressure-lowering effects in clinical studies. Its hypotensive action is attributed to the flavonoids and alkaloids present in the plant, making it a supportive therapy for mild to moderate hypertension.
Thyroid Regulation: Some studies suggest that Shankhapushpi extracts may modulate thyroid hormone levels, particularly in hyperthyroid conditions, by reducing T3 and T4 concentrations.

Chemical Constituents: Contains shankhapushpine (an alkaloid), convolamine, convoline, convosine, confoline (alkaloids), scopoletin (a coumarin), β-sitosterol, kaempferol (a flavonoid), and various triterpenoids including shankhapushpin. These compounds synergistically contribute to its potent Medhya (nootropic) activity.
plantProfile: Unique History: Shankhapushpi derives its name from its flowers, which resemble a 'Shankha' (conch shell). It occupies the highest position among the four Medhya Rasayana herbs listed by Acharya Charaka in the Charaka Samhita (the other three being Brahmi, Mandukaparni/Gotu Kola, and Yashtimadhu/Licorice). Its cerebral tonic properties have been documented for over 3,000 years in classical Ayurvedic texts.

Physical Characteristics: A small, prostrate, branched, perennial herb that creeps along the ground. It has simple, alternately arranged, elliptic to oblong-lanceolate leaves with hairy surfaces. The flowers are small, white or light pink, trumpet-shaped (characteristic of the morning glory family), that bloom singly from the leaf axils. The entire plant is covered with fine, silvery hairs. It typically grows in sandy, dry, and open habitats across India, particularly in Rajasthan, Gujarat, and other semi-arid regions.

Specific Chemical Compounds: Major bioactive constituents include tropane alkaloids (shankhapushpine, convolamine, convoline, confoline, convosine), scopoletin (a coumarin with anti-inflammatory and smooth muscle relaxant properties), flavonoids (kaempferol, quercetin), phytosterols (β-sitosterol), and triterpenoids. The alkaloids, particularly shankhapushpine, are primarily responsible for its neuroprotective and nootropic effects, acting through multiple neurotransmitter pathways.

Strict Standardizations: As per the Ayurvedic Pharmacopoeia of India (API), the whole plant (Panchanga) of Convolvulus pluricaulis is standardized. Note: There is a recognized controversy in identification, as other species (Evolvulus alsinoides and Clitoria ternatea) are also sold as Shankhapushpi in different regions. API specifies C. pluricaulis as the authentic source for North Indian Shankhapushpi. Quality parameters include total ash, acid-insoluble ash, alcohol-soluble extractive, and water-soluble extractive. HPTLC fingerprinting is recommended for species-level identification.
preparationMethods: Powder (Churna): The whole plant (Panchanga) is shade-dried, then finely powdered and sieved through an 80-100 mesh. Consumed with warm milk and honey (or ghee) for brain tonic effects. This combination of Anupana (vehicle) enhances its Medhya potency.

Fresh Juice (Svarasa): The entire fresh plant is crushed to extract juice. This is considered the most potent form for Medhya action. Dose: 10-20ml, typically consumed with honey in the morning on an empty stomach.

Syrup: Commercially available Shankhapushpi Syrup is a popular modern preparation, especially for children. It contains an aqueous extract of the whole plant, often combined with other Medhya herbs like Brahmi.

Medicated Ghee (Shankhapushpi Ghrita): The fresh juice or decoction of Shankhapushpi is cooked with cow's ghee and other herbs as per classical Sneha Kalpana (medicated fat preparation) methods. Ghee acts as a supreme Anupana for brain tonics, as it can cross the blood-brain barrier, delivering lipid-soluble active compounds directly to neural tissue.

Decoction (Kwatha): 10-15g of dried whole plant is boiled in 200ml water and reduced to 50ml. This is less commonly used than the powder or juice for Medhya purposes, but is effective for anxiolytic and anti-hypertensive applications.

dosages.Children: Shankhapushpi is widely used for children in Ayurvedic pediatric practice to improve intellect and memory. Powder: 1-3 grams with warm milk and honey (dose varies by age). Syrup: 5-10ml, 1-2 times daily (as per product label). Fresh Juice: 5-10ml with honey.
dosages.Adults: Powder (Churna): 3-6 grams per day, in divided doses, with warm milk or honey. Fresh Juice (Svarasa): 10-20 ml, 1-2 times daily. Shankhapushpi Ghrita: 5-10 grams once or twice daily.
dosages.Elderly: Standard adult dosages. It is highly recommended for elderly individuals to support cognitive function and prevent age-related memory decline. Start at lower end of dosage range and titrate up. Particularly beneficial when combined with Brahmi.

safetyProfile: Contraindications: Due to its Sheeta Virya (cooling potency), it should be used cautiously in individuals with severe Kapha aggravation or low digestive fire (Mandagni), as it may further suppress Agni. Pregnant and lactating women should consult an Ayurvedic physician before use, as some traditional texts suggest caution during pregnancy due to its potential effect on uterine smooth muscle.

GRAS Status: Shankhapushpi has a robust history of safe, long-term use in traditional Ayurvedic medicine, including in pediatric practice. It is recognized by the Ministry of AYUSH and listed in the API. No significant toxicity has been reported in traditional use or in standard toxicological animal studies at therapeutic doses.

Herb-Drug Interactions: It may potentiate the effects of anti-epileptic drugs (AEDs), benzodiazepines, and other CNS depressants. It has been reported to interfere with the bioavailability and pharmacokinetics of Phenytoin. Patients on thyroid medications should be monitored, as it may influence thyroid hormone levels. Caution with anti-hypertensive drugs due to potential additive blood-pressure-lowering effects.

Adverse Effects: Generally very well-tolerated. In rare cases, high doses may cause mild drowsiness, reduced appetite, or stomach upset, especially in individuals with a cold constitution (Kapha Prakriti) or weak Agni.
